title: Jam Roly-Poly
subtitle: Suet pastry rolled around raspberry jam and steamed until glossy
excerpt: A flat sheet of suet pastry spread with raspberry jam, rolled up, wrapped in greased foil, and steamed for 90 minutes. The suet softens and the jam seeps through the layers. Served in thick slices with custard.
sourceNotes: Jam roly-poly appears in Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) as one of the standard suet-pastry puddings in the British canon. The rolled-up format with jam filling was common in Victorian household cooking as a way to stretch a small amount of jam across many servings.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the uncooked roly-poly wrapped in its foil. Steam from frozen for 2 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make and refrigerate uncooked for up to 24 hours. Steam as normal when ready.

content.text: Suet pastry is easier than shortcrust and more forgiving than any butter-based pastry. There's no need to keep it cold, no resting time, and no crumbling if you handle it too much. Mix flour, suet, and enough milk to bring it together, then roll it out on a floured surface. The rolling doesn't need to be precise: a rough rectangle about 25 × 30 cm is all you need.

The jam wants to be spread generously but not so close to the edges that it leaks out when the roll is steamed. Leave a 2 cm border on all sides and a wider 4 cm border at one short end so the spiral seals properly. Strawberry or apricot jam work in place of raspberry.

items.prepNote: plus a little more if needed

content.text: Stir {{self-raising-flour}} of self-raising flour, {{suet-shredded}} of shredded suet, and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar together in a large bowl. Add {{whole-milk}} of milk gradually, stirring with a knife until the dough comes together in a soft but not sticky ball. Add a splash more milk if it's too dry to come together.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and roll to a rectangle about 25 × 30 cm. Spread {{raspberry-jam}} of raspberry jam over the surface, leaving a 2 cm border on the long sides and a 4 cm border at one short end. Starting from the other short end, roll up firmly but not tightly. Press the sealed edge together and pinch the two short ends closed.

Butter a large sheet of foil generously. Place the roly-poly seam-side down on the foil and wrap it loosely, making a large pleat along the top so the pudding has room to expand. Twist the ends closed like a Christmas cracker. Place on a trivet or upturned saucer in a large saucepan and pour in boiling water to come 5 cm up the sides. Cover the pan and steam over a steady simmer for 1 hour 30 minutes, topping up with boiling water as needed.
Lift from the water and rest in the foil for 5 minutes. Unwrap carefully, steam escapes from the ends. Transfer to a board and cut into 2, 3 cm slices. Serve with warm custard.

items.cause: Edges were not sealed, or jam was spread too close to the border
items.fix: Leave a wider border (4 cm at the sealed short end) and press the spiral firmly at the seam. The suet pastry should feel slightly tacky when pressed.

items.cause: Understeamed, or the pan went off the boil
items.fix: Check at 90 minutes and steam for a further 20 minutes if the pastry still looks pale and raw at the ends. Keep the pan at a steady simmer.

items.cause: The pastry was too dry, or the roll wasn't sealed tightly enough
items.fix: Rest in the foil for the full 5 minutes before unwrapping. Slice with a sharp serrated knife, not a knife with a pushing motion.

items.symptom: Pastry was sticky and difficult to roll
items.cause: Too much milk, or the suet was warm and releasing fat
items.fix: Dust the surface and the dough lightly with flour. Cold suet pastry handles better than warm.

content.text: Jam roly-poly has been on the British pudding map since at least the early Victorian period, appearing in Mrs Beeton's household management manual and in cookery books aimed at economy-minded householders. It belongs to the larger family of suet-pastry puddings, Sussex pond pudding, spotted dick, jam roly-poly, that made substantial, cheap desserts from pantry staples. The pudding's reputation declined through the second half of the twentieth century as home cooking moved away from suet and towards lighter desserts, but the dish has outlasted the fashion.
